title: Thirty-one pages
summary: A young translator in Tbilisi is eight months into a project nobody asked her to do: a book her grandmother wrote in 1954. She does not yet know what the project actually is.
body:
Maya is twenty-six. She has lived in Tbilisi for eight months. She is in Georgia for one reason: to learn Georgian well enough to translate one short book. The book was written by her grandmother in 1954. The book is one hundred and forty pages long. The first thirty pages are about a small village near Mtskheta. The rest is in a Georgian her grandmother spoke as a child and that almost nobody speaks anymore.

Maya does not know exactly why she is doing this. Nobody is paying her. The book has not been requested by a publisher. Her grandmother died in 1998, two years before Maya was born. The Georgian is a language Maya did not grow up with. Her parents speak it together but did not speak it to her.

What she has is a notebook, a small flat, an old dictionary from 1962, and her grandmother's manuscript in a wooden box. She works at the flat's kitchen table from seven in the morning to noon. Then she walks. Then she works from two until six.

She has translated thirty-one pages in eight months.

A friend in London asked her, last week, whether the project was 'going well.' Maya thought about the question for a long time. She wrote back: 'It is not the right question. The right question is whether I am the right person to do this. I do not know that yet. I will know in another year. The project will tell me, not the other way around.'

What Maya has discovered, in those thirty-one pages, is that her grandmother had a sense of humour she has never heard about. There is a joke on page seventeen about a chicken and a priest. Maya cannot translate it cleanly. She has tried twelve times.

She has stopped trying to translate the joke. She has started writing, in the margin, what her grandmother would have laughed at. That, she thinks, may be the actual project. She does not say this to anyone yet. She is afraid the project will turn out to be something else again in three months.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with quantified outcomes
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng hiện tại hoàn thành ('has/have + V3') để kể kết quả tích luỹ đến hiện tại. Trong bài: 'She has translated thirty-one pages in eight months.' (kết quả định lượng). 'She has tried twelve times.' (số lần). 'She has stopped trying to translate the joke. She has started writing in the margin.' (chuỗi hành động có kết quả hiện tại). 'She has lived in Tbilisi for eight months.' (kéo dài đến nay). Cấu trúc này khác quá khứ đơn — nó kết nối quá khứ với hiện tại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses the present perfect ('has/have + past participle') to report cumulative results up to now. The passage: 'She has translated thirty-one pages in eight months.' (quantified result). 'She has tried twelve times.' (count of attempts). 'She has stopped trying… She has started writing in the margin.' (a chain of moves with present consequences). 'She has lived in Tbilisi for eight months.' (still living there). The present perfect, unlike past simple, ties the past to the current moment.

translationVi:
Maya hai mươi sáu tuổi. Cô đã sống ở Tbilisi tám tháng. Cô ở Georgia vì một lý do: học tiếng Georgia đủ giỏi để dịch một cuốn sách ngắn. Cuốn sách do bà ngoại cô viết năm 1954. Sách dày một trăm bốn mươi trang. Ba mươi trang đầu kể về một làng nhỏ gần Mtskheta. Phần còn lại bằng một thứ tiếng Georgia mà bà cô nói hồi còn nhỏ và gần như không ai nói nữa.

Maya không thật sự biết vì sao cô làm điều này. Không ai trả tiền cho cô. Không nhà xuất bản nào đặt sách này. Bà ngoại cô mất năm 1998, hai năm trước khi Maya ra đời. Tiếng Georgia là một ngôn ngữ Maya không lớn lên cùng. Cha mẹ cô nói tiếng đó với nhau nhưng không nói với cô.

Cái cô có là một quyển sổ, một căn hộ nhỏ, một quyển từ điển cũ từ 1962, và bản thảo của bà trong một cái hộp gỗ. Cô làm việc ở bàn bếp căn hộ từ bảy giờ sáng đến trưa. Rồi cô đi bộ. Rồi cô làm việc từ hai giờ đến sáu giờ.

Cô đã dịch ba mươi mốt trang trong tám tháng.

Một người bạn ở London tuần trước hỏi cô dự án có 'ổn không'. Maya nghĩ rất lâu về câu hỏi. Cô viết lại: 'Đó không phải câu hỏi đúng. Câu hỏi đúng là liệu mình có phải người phù hợp để làm việc này không. Mình chưa biết. Mình sẽ biết trong một năm nữa. Dự án sẽ nói cho mình, không phải ngược lại.'

Điều Maya khám phá trong ba mươi mốt trang đó là bà cô có một khiếu hài hước mà cô chưa bao giờ được nghe kể. Có một câu đùa ở trang mười bảy về một con gà và một linh mục. Maya không thể dịch sạch sẽ. Cô đã thử mười hai lần.

Cô đã thôi cố dịch câu đùa đó. Cô đã bắt đầu viết, bên lề, điều mà bà cô có lẽ đã cười. Cô nghĩ đó có thể là dự án thực sự. Cô chưa nói điều này với ai. Cô sợ ba tháng nữa dự án sẽ lại biến thành một thứ khác.

questions.prompt: How old is Maya, and how long has she lived in Tbilisi?

options: To learn Georgian well enough to translate one short book her grandmother wrote in 1954.

questions.prompt: What is unusual about the Georgian in the book?
options: It is a version her grandmother spoke as a child; almost nobody speaks it anymore.
It is in another language entirely.

questions.prompt: What does she have to work with?
options: A notebook, a small flat, an old 1962 dictionary, and her grandmother's manuscript in a wooden box.

questions.prompt: How many pages has she translated in eight months?
questions.explanation: 'She has translated thirty-one pages in eight months.'
questions.hint: Look for the line by itself.

questions.prompt: What did Maya say to her London friend?
options: 'It is not the right question. The right question is whether I am the right person to do this.'

questions.prompt: What did she discover on page seventeen?
options: A joke about a chicken and a priest — she could not translate it cleanly; she has tried twelve times.
questions.explanation: 'There is a joke on page seventeen about a chicken and a priest.'

questions.prompt: What has she started writing in the margin instead?
options: What her grandmother would have laughed at.
questions.explanation: 'She has started writing, in the margin, what her grandmother would have laughed at.'

questions.prompt: Why doesn't she tell anyone about this new project?
options: She is afraid the project will turn out to be something else again in three months.
She does not have a phone.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the passage NOT claiming?
options: Maya is being paid for the work.
The project may turn out to be different than originally imagined.
Maya's grandmother died before Maya was born.
Maya did not grow up speaking Georgian.
questions.hint: Look at who funds the work.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A long, private translation project may slowly reveal a different real goal than the one the translator started with — and that is part of the work, not a failure.
Georgian is too hard for translation.
Grandparents' books should not be touched.
Translation should be a paid profession only.
questions.explanation: The passage's quoted email and the margin notes reframe the project.
questions.hint: Notice how Maya redefines the project.

cloze.template:
Maya is twenty-six. She has lived in Tbilisi for {{b1}} months. She is in Georgia for one reason: to learn Georgian well enough to {{b2}} one short book. The book was written by her grandmother in {{b3}}. The book is one hundred and forty pages long.

Maya does not know exactly why she is doing this. {{b4}} is paying her. The book has not been requested by a publisher. Her grandmother died in 1998, two years before Maya was born. The Georgian is a language Maya did not grow up with.

What she has is a notebook, a small flat, an old {{b5}} from 1962, and her grandmother's manuscript in a wooden box. She works at the flat's kitchen table from seven in the morning to noon. Then she walks. Then she works from two until six.

She has translated thirty-{{b6}} pages in eight months.

A friend in London asked her, last week, whether the project was 'going well.' Maya thought about the question for a long time. She wrote back: 'It is not the right question. The right question is whether I am the right {{b7}} to do this. I do not know that yet. I will know in another year. The project will tell me, not the other way around.'

What Maya has discovered, in those thirty-one pages, is that her grandmother had a sense of {{b8}} she has never heard about. There is a joke on page seventeen about a chicken and a priest. Maya cannot translate it cleanly. She has tried {{b9}} times.

She has stopped trying to translate the joke. She has started writing, in the {{b10}}, what her grandmother would have laughed at. That, she thinks, may be the actual project. She does not say this to anyone yet. She is afraid the project will turn out to be something {{b11}} again in three months.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Maya reframes the question from 'Is the project going well?' to 'Am I the right person to do this?' Is there a long-running project in your own life — a relationship, a career, a creative work — where the better question is about you, not about progress? What would the honest answer be today?
